title: Criminal Law
subject: Criminal Law
summary: Criminal Law covers criminal liability, homicide, property crimes, inchoate offenses, defenses, and party liability.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: A voluntary act or legally punishable omission
Concurrence between act and mental state
Actual and proximate causation when a result is required
Proof of every element beyond a reasonable doubt
The MBE may apply common law, a modern statutory rule, the Model Penal Code, or a statute supplied in the question.

blocks.text: A voluntary act or legally punishable omission
Concurrence between act and mental state
Actual and proximate causation when a result is required
Proof of every element beyond a reasonable doubt
The MBE may apply common law, a modern statutory rule, the Model Penal Code, or a statute supplied in the question.

sections.title: 1. Criminal Law Attack Plan
body: Is the defendant a principal, accomplice, conspirator, or accessory after the fact?
Does a defense negate, justify, or excuse?
Do merger rules limit conviction or punishment?
Memory line: Act, mind, cause, crime, party, defense, merger.

blocks.text: Is the defendant a principal, accomplice, conspirator, or accessory after the fact?
Does a defense negate, justify, or excuse?
Do merger rules limit conviction or punishment?
Memory line: Act, mind, cause, crime, party, defense, merger.

sections.title: 2. Actus Reus
body: A crime generally requires a voluntary physical act.
A bad thought alone is not punishable.
Speech may be the act when the offense is committed through words, such as solicitation, perjury, extortion, fraud, or threats.
Possession may count when the defendant knowingly controls the item long enough to terminate possession.

blocks.text: A crime generally requires a voluntary physical act.
A bad thought alone is not punishable.
Speech may be the act when the offense is committed through words, such as solicitation, perjury, extortion, fraud, or threats.
Possession may count when the defendant knowingly controls the item long enough to terminate possession.

sections.title: 3. Involuntary Conduct
body: The following are generally not voluntary acts:
Acts caused by direct physical compulsion
A defendant may still be liable when an earlier voluntary act created the danger.
Example: A person who knows uncontrolled seizures are likely chooses to drive and later causes a fatal collision during a seizure.
Memory line: Look backward for the last voluntary act.

blocks.text: The following are generally not voluntary acts:
Acts caused by direct physical compulsion
A defendant may still be liable when an earlier voluntary act created the danger.
Example: A person who knows uncontrolled seizures are likely chooses to drive and later causes a fatal collision during a seizure.
Memory line: Look backward for the last voluntary act.

sections.title: 4. Omissions
body: Failure to act is criminal only when:
The defendant knows the facts creating the duty
The omission causes the prohibited result
A mere bystander generally has no duty to rescue.

blocks.text: Failure to act is criminal only when:
The defendant knows the facts creating the duty
The omission causes the prohibited result
A mere bystander generally has no duty to rescue.

sections.title: 5. Concurrence
body: The required mens rea must exist when the defendant commits the actus reus.
A later guilty thought ordinarily cannot transform an innocent act into a crime unless a doctrine such as continuing trespass applies.

blocks.text: The required mens rea must exist when the defendant commits the actus reus.
A later guilty thought ordinarily cannot transform an innocent act into a crime unless a doctrine such as continuing trespass applies.

sections.title: 6. Common-Law Mens Rea Categories
body: Assault with intent to commit battery
Specific intent matters because voluntary intoxication and an unreasonable mistake of fact may negate it.
Common-law murder and arson are malice crimes.
Malice does not mean hatred. It includes intentional wrongdoing or reckless disregard of a high risk.
Memory line: FIAT is specific; murder and arson are malice; ordinary intentional acts are general intent.

blocks.text: Assault with intent to commit battery
Specific intent matters because voluntary intoxication and an unreasonable mistake of fact may negate it.
Common-law murder and arson are malice crimes.
Malice does not mean hatred. It includes intentional wrongdoing or reckless disregard of a high risk.
Memory line: FIAT is specific; murder and arson are malice; ordinary intentional acts are general intent.

sections.title: 7. Motive
body: Motive explains why the defendant acted.
Mens rea identifies the legally required mental state.
A sympathetic motive does not automatically eliminate criminal intent.

blocks.text: Motive explains why the defendant acted.
Mens rea identifies the legally required mental state.
A sympathetic motive does not automatically eliminate criminal intent.

sections.title: 8. MPC Mental States
body: Recklessly — conscious disregard of a substantial and unjustifiable risk
Negligently — failure to perceive a substantial and unjustifiable risk
Proof of a higher level generally satisfies a lower one.
When the MPC statute is silent, recklessness is usually sufficient.

blocks.text: Recklessly — conscious disregard of a substantial and unjustifiable risk
Negligently — failure to perceive a substantial and unjustifiable risk
Proof of a higher level generally satisfies a lower one.
When the MPC statute is silent, recklessness is usually sufficient.

sections.title: 9. Strict Liability
body: Strict-liability crimes require no mens rea for the relevant element.
Mistake of fact is generally no defense.
Courts disfavor strict liability for serious crimes carrying severe punishment.

blocks.text: Strict-liability crimes require no mens rea for the relevant element.
Mistake of fact is generally no defense.
Courts disfavor strict liability for serious crimes carrying severe punishment.

sections.title: 10. Vicarious and Corporate Liability
body: Vicarious liability attributes another person’s criminal act to the defendant.
An employee acts within the scope of employment
A high-ranking agent authorizes or recklessly tolerates the conduct
The corporation violates a legal duty
The responsible individual may also be convicted.

blocks.text: Vicarious liability attributes another person’s criminal act to the defendant.
An employee acts within the scope of employment
A high-ranking agent authorizes or recklessly tolerates the conduct
The corporation violates a legal duty
The responsible individual may also be convicted.

sections.title: 11. Mistake of Fact
body: Specific intent — Honest, even if unreasonable
MPC — Must negate the required mental state
The defendant bears a burden of production, but the prosecution retains the burden to prove mens rea.

blocks.text: Specific intent — Honest, even if unreasonable
MPC — Must negate the required mental state
The defendant bears a burden of production, but the prosecution retains the burden to prove mens rea.

sections.title: 12. Mistake of Law
body: Ignorance of the law is no excuse.
The offense requires knowledge of illegality
Reasonable reliance on an official interpretation later found wrong
The law was not reasonably available for a malum prohibitum offense
The mistake negates a separate required mental element
Reliance on private legal advice ordinarily is not itself a complete defense.

blocks.text: Ignorance of the law is no excuse.
The offense requires knowledge of illegality
Reasonable reliance on an official interpretation later found wrong
The law was not reasonably available for a malum prohibitum offense
The mistake negates a separate required mental element
Reliance on private legal advice ordinarily is not itself a complete defense.

sections.title: 13. Transferred Intent
body: Intent transfers when the defendant aims at one victim but causes the same type of completed harm to another.
It does not create attempted murder of the unintended victim.
Example: A shoots at B, misses, and wounds C. A may be guilty of attempted murder of B and battery of C, but not attempted murder of C.

blocks.text: Intent transfers when the defendant aims at one victim but causes the same type of completed harm to another.
It does not create attempted murder of the unintended victim.
Example: A shoots at B, misses, and wounds C. A may be guilty of attempted murder of B and battery of C, but not attempted murder of C.

sections.title: 14. Principals
body: A principal directly commits the actus reus, acts through an innocent instrumentality, or is constructively present through a controlled mechanism.
Multiple direct actors may be joint principals.

blocks.text: A principal directly commits the actus reus, acts through an innocent instrumentality, or is constructively present through a controlled mechanism.
Multiple direct actors may be joint principals.

sections.title: 15. Accomplice Liability
body: Aids, encourages, solicits, or attempts to aid
The majority and MPC generally require the purpose of promoting or facilitating the crime.
Mere presence or knowledge is not enough.

blocks.text: Aids, encourages, solicits, or attempts to aid
The majority and MPC generally require the purpose of promoting or facilitating the crime.
Mere presence or knowledge is not enough.

sections.title: 16. Scope of Accomplice Liability
body: Traditionally, an accomplice is liable for:
Additional crimes that are natural and probable consequences
The MPC is narrower and generally requires accomplice culpability for each offense.
A person protected by the statute ordinarily cannot be an accomplice to the offense against that protected class.

blocks.text: Traditionally, an accomplice is liable for:
Additional crimes that are natural and probable consequences
The MPC is narrower and generally requires accomplice culpability for each offense.
A person protected by the statute ordinarily cannot be an accomplice to the offense against that protected class.

sections.title: 17. Accomplice Withdrawal
body: Acting before the crime becomes unstoppable
Simply leaving or changing one’s mind is insufficient.

blocks.text: Acting before the crime becomes unstoppable
Simply leaving or changing one’s mind is insufficient.

sections.title: 18. Accessory After the Fact
body: Helps the felon avoid apprehension, trial, or punishment
This is a separate offense, not liability for the original felony.

blocks.text: Helps the felon avoid apprehension, trial, or punishment
This is a separate offense, not liability for the original felony.

sections.title: 19. Insanity Tests
body: M’Naghten — Defendant did not know the nature of the act or that it was wrong
Irresistible impulse — Defendant could not control conduct or conform to law
Durham — Crime was the product of mental disease or defect
MPC — Defendant lacked substantial capacity to appreciate wrongfulness or conform conduct
Insanity concerns the defendant’s condition at the time of the crime.
Competency concerns present ability to understand proceedings and assist counsel.

blocks.text: M’Naghten — Defendant did not know the nature of the act or that it was wrong
Irresistible impulse — Defendant could not control conduct or conform to law
Durham — Crime was the product of mental disease or defect
MPC — Defendant lacked substantial capacity to appreciate wrongfulness or conform conduct
Insanity concerns the defendant’s condition at the time of the crime.
Competency concerns present ability to understand proceedings and assist counsel.

sections.title: 20. Intoxication
body: May negate specific intent or, under the MPC, purpose or knowledge.
It is not a defense to:
May negate mens rea, eliminate a voluntary act, or satisfy an insanity test.
Memory line: Voluntary intoxication is narrow; involuntary intoxication is broad.

blocks.text: May negate specific intent or, under the MPC, purpose or knowledge.
It is not a defense to:
May negate mens rea, eliminate a voluntary act, or satisfy an insanity test.
Memory line: Voluntary intoxication is narrow; involuntary intoxication is broad.

sections.title: 21. Infancy
body: Age 14 and older: treated as capable
Modern statutes control in actual jurisdictions.

blocks.text: Age 14 and older: treated as capable
Modern statutes control in actual jurisdictions.

sections.title: 22. Homicide Framework
body: Did the defendant actually cause death?
Did the defendant proximately cause death?

blocks.text: Did the defendant actually cause death?
Did the defendant proximately cause death?

sections.title: 23. Actual Causation
body: Actual cause generally requires but-for causation.
When two independent acts are each sufficient to cause death, each may be treated as a substantial factor.
The defendant takes the victim as found. A preexisting weakness does not break causation.

blocks.text: Actual cause generally requires but-for causation.
When two independent acts are each sufficient to cause death, each may be treated as a substantial factor.
The defendant takes the victim as found. A preexisting weakness does not break causation.

sections.title: 24. Proximate Causation
body: Death must be a natural and probable consequence of the defendant’s conduct.
An intervening event generally breaks causation only when it is extraordinary.
Ordinary medical negligence, foreseeable rescue, and reasonable victim escape usually do not break the chain.

blocks.text: Death must be a natural and probable consequence of the defendant’s conduct.
An intervening event generally breaks causation only when it is extraordinary.
Ordinary medical negligence, foreseeable rescue, and reasonable victim escape usually do not break the chain.

sections.title: 25. Common-Law Murder
body: Murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.
Intent to cause serious bodily injury

blocks.text: Murder is the unlawful killing of another human being with malice aforethought.
Intent to cause serious bodily injury

sections.title: 26. Depraved-Heart Murder
body: This is an unintentional killing caused by conscious disregard of an extremely high risk to human life.
It requires greater blameworthiness than involuntary manslaughter.
Example: Firing repeatedly into an occupied building without aiming at a particular person.

blocks.text: This is an unintentional killing caused by conscious disregard of an extremely high risk to human life.
It requires greater blameworthiness than involuntary manslaughter.
Example: Firing repeatedly into an occupied building without aiming at a particular person.

sections.title: 27. Felony Murder
body: Felony murder is an unintended killing during an inherently dangerous felony.
Death during the felony or immediate flight

blocks.text: Felony murder is an unintended killing during an inherently dangerous felony.
Death during the felony or immediate flight

sections.title: 28. Felony-Murder Merger
body: An assaultive felony that directly causes the death generally merges into the homicide and cannot serve as the independent predicate felony.
Memory line: The felony must be independent of the killing itself.

blocks.text: An assaultive felony that directly causes the death generally merges into the homicide and cannot serve as the independent predicate felony.
Memory line: The felony must be independent of the killing itself.

sections.title: 29. Police or Victim Killings During a Felony
body: Majority rule: a felon is generally not liable for felony murder when police or the victim fires the fatal shot.
Minority rule: liability may attach if the death was a foreseeable consequence of the felony.
Apply agency theory unless the question specifies otherwise.

blocks.text: Majority rule: a felon is generally not liable for felony murder when police or the victim fires the fatal shot.
Minority rule: liability may attach if the death was a foreseeable consequence of the felony.
Apply agency theory unless the question specifies otherwise.

sections.title: 30. First-Degree Murder
body: Heinous methods such as torture, poison, or lying in wait
Premeditation may be brief, but actual reflection is required.

blocks.text: Heinous methods such as torture, poison, or lying in wait
Premeditation may be brief, but actual reflection is required.

sections.title: 31. Second-Degree Murder
body: Second-degree murder generally includes murder not classified as first degree, including:

blocks.text: Second-degree murder generally includes murder not classified as first degree, including:

sections.title: 32. Voluntary Manslaughter
body: Voluntary manslaughter is an intentional killing mitigated by:
Adequate provocation and heat of passion

blocks.text: Voluntary manslaughter is an intentional killing mitigated by:
Adequate provocation and heat of passion

sections.title: 33. Heat of Passion
body: Causal connection between provocation, passion, and killing
Mere insulting words are traditionally insufficient.

blocks.text: Causal connection between provocation, passion, and killing
Mere insulting words are traditionally insufficient.

sections.title: 34. Imperfect Self-Defense
body: An honest but unreasonable belief in the need for deadly force may reduce murder to voluntary manslaughter in jurisdictions recognizing the doctrine.
It mitigates but does not justify.

blocks.text: An honest but unreasonable belief in the need for deadly force may reduce murder to voluntary manslaughter in jurisdictions recognizing the doctrine.
It mitigates but does not justify.

sections.title: 35. Involuntary Manslaughter
body: Involuntary manslaughter is an unintentional killing caused by:
A felony not supporting felony murder
Criminal negligence is more serious than ordinary tort negligence.

blocks.text: Involuntary manslaughter is an unintentional killing caused by:
A felony not supporting felony murder
Criminal negligence is more serious than ordinary tort negligence.

sections.title: 36. Larceny
body: Memory line: Wrongful possession change + movement + intent to steal.

blocks.text: Memory line: Wrongful possession change + movement + intent to steal.

sections.title: 37. Caption and Asportation
body: The MPC generally focuses on unauthorized control rather than movement.

blocks.text: The MPC generally focuses on unauthorized control rather than movement.

sections.title: 38. Property of Another
body: A title owner may steal from a lawful possessor
A thief may have possession protected against a stranger
A joint owner ordinarily cannot steal jointly owned property from a co-owner

blocks.text: A title owner may steal from a lawful possessor
A thief may have possession protected against a stranger
A joint owner ordinarily cannot steal jointly owned property from a co-owner

sections.title: 39. Employees: Custody vs. Possession
body: A low-level employee usually has custody, while the employer retains possession.
Misappropriation by the low-level employee is usually larceny.
A high-level employee may have lawful possession, making misappropriation embezzlement.

blocks.text: A low-level employee usually has custody, while the employer retains possession.
Misappropriation by the low-level employee is usually larceny.
A high-level employee may have lawful possession, making misappropriation embezzlement.

sections.title: 40. Lost and Abandoned Property
body: Lost property can be larceny when, at the time of finding:
The finder knows or can identify the owner
The finder intends to keep it permanently
Mistaken delivery follows the same timing principle.

blocks.text: Lost property can be larceny when, at the time of finding:
The finder knows or can identify the owner
The finder intends to keep it permanently
Mistaken delivery follows the same timing principle.

sections.title: 41. Intent to Permanently Deprive
body: Intent exists when the defendant plans to:
Pawn it without ability to redeem
Create a substantial risk of permanent loss
Temporary borrowing with ability and intent to return generally is not larceny.

blocks.text: Intent exists when the defendant plans to:
Pawn it without ability to redeem
Create a substantial risk of permanent loss
Temporary borrowing with ability and intent to return generally is not larceny.

sections.title: 42. Claim of Right
body: An honest belief that the specific property belongs to the defendant negates larcenous intent.
Claim of right is more limited for robbery because modern law disfavors forcible self-help.

blocks.text: An honest belief that the specific property belongs to the defendant negates larcenous intent.
Claim of right is more limited for robbery because modern law disfavors forcible self-help.

sections.title: 43. Continuing Trespass
body: When the original taking is wrongful but lacks intent to steal, later intent may satisfy concurrence because the trespass is treated as continuing.
The original taking must have been wrongful.

blocks.text: When the original taking is wrongful but lacks intent to steal, later intent may satisfy concurrence because the trespass is treated as continuing.
The original taking must have been wrongful.

sections.title: 44. Larceny by Trick
body: Larceny by trick occurs when fraud causes the victim to transfer possession but not title.
The victim must actually rely on the material misrepresentation.

blocks.text: Larceny by trick occurs when fraud causes the victim to transfer possession but not title.
The victim must actually rely on the material misrepresentation.

sections.title: 45. False Pretenses
body: False pretenses occurs when fraud causes the victim to transfer title.

blocks.text: False pretenses occurs when fraud causes the victim to transfer title.

sections.title: 46. Embezzlement
body: Embezzlement is fraudulent conversion of property of another by one already in lawful possession.
Intent to return equivalent property or money does not excuse conversion of the specific entrusted property.

blocks.text: Embezzlement is fraudulent conversion of property of another by one already in lawful possession.
Intent to return equivalent property or money does not excuse conversion of the specific entrusted property.

sections.title: 47. Forgery
body: The writing itself must be false, not merely contain false information.

blocks.text: The writing itself must be false, not merely contain false information.

sections.title: 48. Robbery
body: The threat generally must involve immediate serious physical harm.
Future or nonphysical threats usually indicate extortion.

blocks.text: The threat generally must involve immediate serious physical harm.
Future or nonphysical threats usually indicate extortion.

sections.title: 49. Force in Robbery
body: Pickpocketing without awareness is usually larceny.
Purse-snatching becomes robbery when additional force:
Exceeds what is needed merely to move the property

blocks.text: Pickpocketing without awareness is usually larceny.
Purse-snatching becomes robbery when additional force:
Exceeds what is needed merely to move the property

sections.title: 50. Extortion
body: Modern extortion involves obtaining or seeking property through threats.
The threat may concern future harm
The threat need not be physical
The property need not be in the victim’s presence

blocks.text: Modern extortion involves obtaining or seeking property through threats.
The threat may concern future harm
The threat need not be physical
The property need not be in the victim’s presence

sections.title: 51. Burglary
body: With intent to commit a felony inside
Modern statutes commonly eliminate breaking, dwelling, or nighttime requirements.

blocks.text: With intent to commit a felony inside
Modern statutes commonly eliminate breaking, dwelling, or nighttime requirements.

sections.title: 52. Breaking and Entry
body: Breaking may be slight, such as opening an unlocked closed door.
Entering through a fully open door is not a common-law breaking.
Any body part crosses the boundary
An instrument used to commit the felony crosses the boundary
The felony intent must exist at entry.

blocks.text: Breaking may be slight, such as opening an unlocked closed door.
Entering through a fully open door is not a common-law breaking.
Any body part crosses the boundary
An instrument used to commit the felony crosses the boundary
The felony intent must exist at entry.

sections.title: 53. Arson
body: Common-law arson is malicious burning of the dwelling of another.
Smoke or scorching alone is insufficient

blocks.text: Common-law arson is malicious burning of the dwelling of another.
Smoke or scorching alone is insufficient

sections.title: 54. Receiving Stolen Property
body: The property must still be stolen when received.

blocks.text: The property must still be stolen when received.

sections.title: 55. Battery
body: Battery is unlawful harmful or offensive contact with another.
Applied to something attached to the victim
Battery is traditionally a general-intent crime.

blocks.text: Battery is unlawful harmful or offensive contact with another.
Applied to something attached to the victim
Battery is traditionally a general-intent crime.

sections.title: 56. Assault
body: Intentional creation of reasonable apprehension of imminent bodily harm
Attempted-battery assault does not require victim awareness.

blocks.text: Intentional creation of reasonable apprehension of imminent bodily harm
Attempted-battery assault does not require victim awareness.

sections.title: 57. Kidnapping and False Imprisonment
body: Movement incidental to another crime generally must exceed what is necessary for that crime.

blocks.text: Movement incidental to another crime generally must exceed what is necessary for that crime.

sections.title: 58. Rape
body: Traditional common-law rape required intercourse with a woman by force or threat and without consent.
Modern statutes are generally gender-neutral and focus on absence of valid consent.
Fraud in the factum may negate consent; fraud in the inducement generally does not.

blocks.text: Traditional common-law rape required intercourse with a woman by force or threat and without consent.
Modern statutes are generally gender-neutral and focus on absence of valid consent.
Fraud in the factum may negate consent; fraud in the inducement generally does not.

sections.title: 59. Statutory Rape
body: Statutory rape is generally strict liability as to the victim’s age.
Consent and reasonable mistake of age are ordinarily not defenses unless the statute provides otherwise.

blocks.text: Statutory rape is generally strict liability as to the victim’s age.
Consent and reasonable mistake of age are ordinarily not defenses unless the statute provides otherwise.

sections.title: 60. Perjury and Bribery
body: Requires a knowingly false, material statement under oath.
Modern bribery is completed by the corrupt offer, giving, solicitation, or receipt of value to influence official or legal action.
The desired action need not occur.

blocks.text: Requires a knowingly false, material statement under oath.
Modern bribery is completed by the corrupt offer, giving, solicitation, or receipt of value to influence official or legal action.
The desired action need not occur.

sections.title: 62. Merger
body: Solicitation merges into the completed offense
Attempt merges into the completed offense
A defendant generally cannot receive multiple punishments for a completed offense and its lesser included offense.

blocks.text: Solicitation merges into the completed offense
Attempt merges into the completed offense
A defendant generally cannot receive multiple punishments for a completed offense and its lesser included offense.

sections.title: 63. Solicitation
body: Solicitation is asking, encouraging, commanding, or enticing another person to commit a crime with intent that the crime occur.
The crime is complete when the request is made.
The other person need not agree.

blocks.text: Solicitation is asking, encouraging, commanding, or enticing another person to commit a crime with intent that the crime occur.
The crime is complete when the request is made.
The other person need not agree.

sections.title: 64. Solicitation Defenses
body: Factual impossibility is not a defense.
At common law, renunciation is not a defense.
Under the MPC, voluntary renunciation may be a defense if the defendant prevents the solicited crime.

blocks.text: Factual impossibility is not a defense.
At common law, renunciation is not a defense.
Under the MPC, voluntary renunciation may be a defense if the defendant prevents the solicited crime.

sections.title: 65. Conspiracy
body: The agreement may be inferred from coordinated conduct.

blocks.text: The agreement may be inferred from coordinated conduct.

sections.title: 66. Bilateral and Unilateral Conspiracy
body: Common law and federal law generally require two genuinely guilty parties.
The MPC and many states permit unilateral conspiracy, including agreement with an undercover officer.

blocks.text: Common law and federal law generally require two genuinely guilty parties.
The MPC and many states permit unilateral conspiracy, including agreement with an undercover officer.

sections.title: 67. Wharton’s Rule
body: When a crime necessarily requires two participants, there is ordinarily no separate conspiracy unless more people agree than are necessary to commit the crime.
The rule generally does not apply under the MPC unilateral approach.

blocks.text: When a crime necessarily requires two participants, there is ordinarily no separate conspiracy unless more people agree than are necessary to commit the crime.
The rule generally does not apply under the MPC unilateral approach.

sections.title: 68. Conspiracy Mens Rea
body: A supplier becomes a conspirator only when facts show shared criminal purpose.

blocks.text: A supplier becomes a conspirator only when facts show shared criminal purpose.

sections.title: 69. Overt Act
body: Common law required no overt act.
Federal law and the modern majority generally do.

blocks.text: Common law required no overt act.
Federal law and the modern majority generally do.

sections.title: 70. Pinkerton Liability
body: Under the traditional rule, a conspirator may be liable for substantive crimes committed by another conspirator when they are:
The MPC generally requires accomplice liability instead.

blocks.text: Under the traditional rule, a conspirator may be liable for substantive crimes committed by another conspirator when they are:
The MPC generally requires accomplice liability instead.

sections.title: 71. Conspiracy Withdrawal
body: Withdrawal ordinarily does not erase liability for an already completed conspiracy.
It may cut off liability for later Pinkerton crimes if the defendant:
Prior accomplice aid may still need to be neutralized.

blocks.text: Withdrawal ordinarily does not erase liability for an already completed conspiracy.
It may cut off liability for later Pinkerton crimes if the defendant:
Prior accomplice aid may still need to be neutralized.

sections.title: 72. Attempt
body: Specific intent to complete the target crime
Attempt is specific intent even when the completed offense is general intent, malice, or strict liability.
There is generally no attempt to commit an unintentional crime.

blocks.text: Specific intent to complete the target crime
Attempt is specific intent even when the completed offense is general intent, malice, or strict liability.
There is generally no attempt to commit an unintentional crime.

sections.title: 73. Substantial Step
body: Under the MPC, a substantial step must strongly corroborate criminal purpose.
Possessing materials near the crime scene
Enticing the victim to the planned location

blocks.text: Under the MPC, a substantial step must strongly corroborate criminal purpose.
Possessing materials near the crime scene
Enticing the victim to the planned location

sections.title: 74. Dangerous Proximity
body: The common-law dangerous-proximity test requires the defendant to be very near completion.
It is stricter than the MPC substantial-step test.
Memory line: Common law asks how close; MPC asks how strongly the act confirms intent.

blocks.text: The common-law dangerous-proximity test requires the defendant to be very near completion.
It is stricter than the MPC substantial-step test.
Memory line: Common law asks how close; MPC asks how strongly the act confirms intent.

sections.title: 75. Impossibility
body: Not a defense to attempt, conspiracy, or solicitation.
A defense when the intended conduct is not criminal.
Example: Picking an empty pocket is attempted larceny. Trying to import an item mistakenly believed illegal is not attempt when importation is lawful.

blocks.text: Not a defense to attempt, conspiracy, or solicitation.
A defense when the intended conduct is not criminal.
Example: Picking an empty pocket is attempted larceny. Trying to import an item mistakenly believed illegal is not attempt when importation is lawful.

sections.title: 76. Abandonment
body: At common law, abandonment is no defense once attempt is complete.
The MPC recognizes voluntary and complete renunciation.
Renunciation is not voluntary when motivated by:

blocks.text: At common law, abandonment is no defense once attempt is complete.
The MPC recognizes voluntary and complete renunciation.
Renunciation is not voluntary when motivated by:

sections.title: 77. Self-Defense
body: A nonaggressor may use reasonable force to prevent imminent unlawful harm.

blocks.text: A nonaggressor may use reasonable force to prevent imminent unlawful harm.

sections.title: 78. Deadly Force
body: Deadly force is justified only when reasonably necessary to prevent:
Deadly force cannot be used solely to protect property.

blocks.text: Deadly force is justified only when reasonably necessary to prevent:
Deadly force cannot be used solely to protect property.

sections.title: 79. Retreat and Castle Doctrine
body: The majority imposes no duty to retreat before using deadly force.
Even retreat jurisdictions generally impose no retreat duty in the home.

blocks.text: The majority imposes no duty to retreat before using deadly force.
Even retreat jurisdictions generally impose no retreat duty in the home.

sections.title: 80. Initial Aggressor
body: An initial aggressor may regain self-defense when:
The aggressor completely withdraws and communicates withdrawal, or
The aggressor used nondeadly force and the victim unexpectedly escalates to deadly force

blocks.text: An initial aggressor may regain self-defense when:
The aggressor completely withdraws and communicates withdrawal, or
The aggressor used nondeadly force and the victim unexpectedly escalates to deadly force

sections.title: 81. Defense of Others
body: A person may use force when reasonably believing another person has the right of self-defense.
The modern rule protects reasonable mistakes.

blocks.text: A person may use force when reasonably believing another person has the right of self-defense.
The modern rule protects reasonable mistakes.

sections.title: 82. Defense of Property
body: Reasonable nondeadly force may protect property from immediate unlawful interference.
Deadly force may sometimes protect an occupied dwelling against a violent felony, but not ordinary property.

blocks.text: Reasonable nondeadly force may protect property from immediate unlawful interference.
Deadly force may sometimes protect an occupied dwelling against a violent felony, but not ordinary property.

sections.title: 83. Arrest
body: Police may use reasonable nondeadly force to make a lawful arrest.
Deadly force generally requires a serious threat to the officer or others.
Citizen arrest rules are stricter and often require that the crime actually occurred.

blocks.text: Police may use reasonable nondeadly force to make a lawful arrest.
Deadly force generally requires a serious threat to the officer or others.
Citizen arrest rules are stricter and often require that the crime actually occurred.

sections.title: 84. Duress
body: Duress is generally not a defense to intentional murder.
It may defeat felony murder by defeating the predicate felony.

blocks.text: Duress is generally not a defense to intentional murder.
It may defeat felony murder by defeating the predicate felony.

sections.title: 85. Necessity
body: Necessity applies when circumstances force the defendant to choose the lesser evil.
Defendant did not create the emergency
Traditional necessity arises from natural forces; the MPC is broader.

blocks.text: Necessity applies when circumstances force the defendant to choose the lesser evil.
Defendant did not create the emergency
Traditional necessity arises from natural forces; the MPC is broader.

sections.title: 86. Consent
body: Not obtained by fraud or duress
Given by a legally competent person
The law permits waiver of the protected interest
Consent generally does not excuse serious bodily harm outside recognized activities.

blocks.text: Not obtained by fraud or duress
Given by a legally competent person
The law permits waiver of the protected interest
Consent generally does not excuse serious bodily harm outside recognized activities.

sections.title: 87. Entrapment
body: Government methods create a substantial risk that an ordinarily law-abiding person would commit the crime.
A private citizen cannot create entrapment.

blocks.text: Government methods create a substantial risk that an ordinarily law-abiding person would commit the crime.
A private citizen cannot create entrapment.

sections.title: 88. Alibi
body: Alibi is not an affirmative defense.
The defendant raises doubt about presence or identity.
The prosecution retains the burden to prove the defendant committed the offense.

blocks.text: Alibi is not an affirmative defense.
The defendant raises doubt about presence or identity.
The prosecution retains the burden to prove the defendant committed the offense.

sections.title: 89. Property-Crime Comparison
body: Crime — Initial Status — What Changes — Method
Larceny by trick — Victim possesses — Possession — Fraud
False pretenses — Victim has title — Title — Fraud
Embezzlement — Defendant lawfully possesses — Conversion — Breach of trust
Robbery — Victim possesses — Possession — Force or intimidation

blocks.text: Crime — Initial Status — What Changes — Method
Larceny by trick — Victim possesses — Possession — Fraud
False pretenses — Victim has title — Title — Fraud
Embezzlement — Defendant lawfully possesses — Conversion — Breach of trust
Robbery — Victim possesses — Possession — Force or intimidation

sections.title: 90. Homicide Comparison
body: Dangerous felony causing death — Felony murder
Malum in se misdemeanor causing death — Involuntary manslaughter

blocks.text: Dangerous felony causing death — Felony murder
Malum in se misdemeanor causing death — Involuntary manslaughter

sections.title: 91. Inchoate Comparison
body: Solicitation — Request or encouragement — Intent crime occur — Merges
Conspiracy — Agreement, often overt act — Intent to agree and achieve objective — Does not merge
Attempt — Substantial step or dangerous proximity — Intent to complete crime — Merges

blocks.text: Solicitation — Request or encouragement — Intent crime occur — Merges
Conspiracy — Agreement, often overt act — Intent to agree and achieve objective — Does not merge
Attempt — Substantial step or dangerous proximity — Intent to complete crime — Merges

sections.title: 92. Core MBE Traps
body: A bad thought is not an act.
A bystander usually has no duty to rescue.
Mere knowledge does not make an accomplice or conspirator.
Accessory after the fact is a separate offense.
Ordinary medical negligence usually does not break homicide causation.
Felony murder needs an independent felony.
Larceny protects possession, not merely title.
Larceny by trick transfers possession; false pretenses transfers title.
Burglary intent must exist at entry.
Solicitation is complete upon the request.
Factual impossibility is not a defense.
Deadly force cannot protect property alone.
Duress comes from human threats; necessity comes from circumstances.
Alibi leaves the burden on the prosecution.

blocks.text: A bad thought is not an act.
A bystander usually has no duty to rescue.
Mere knowledge does not make an accomplice or conspirator.
Accessory after the fact is a separate offense.
Ordinary medical negligence usually does not break homicide causation.
Felony murder needs an independent felony.
Larceny protects possession, not merely title.
Larceny by trick transfers possession; false pretenses transfers title.
Burglary intent must exist at entry.
Solicitation is complete upon the request.
Factual impossibility is not a defense.
Deadly force cannot protect property alone.
Duress comes from human threats; necessity comes from circumstances.
Alibi leaves the burden on the prosecution.

sections.title: Final One-Sentence Summary
body: Criminal law asks whether a voluntary act, required guilty mind, and legally caused harm satisfy an offense, and whether party liability, inchoate doctrines, or a defense changes the result.

blocks.text: Criminal law asks whether a voluntary act, required guilty mind, and legally caused harm satisfy an offense, and whether party liability, inchoate doctrines, or a defense changes the result.
